data.title: RLX Technology: Turning Positive On M&A And New Products (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I've become bullish on RLX Technology (NYSE:RLX). It has taken steps to realize its growth potential in new geographical markets and product segments.

My prior August 21, 2024, article touched on the mixed read-throughs from RLX's 2Q24 disclosures.

Positive On Inorganic Expansion

At its 2Q25 briefing on Aug. 22, RLX highlighted the "consolidation of our recently acquired European e-vapor company in June." My take is that this is a wise move. Its new subsidiary is "UK vape retailer Totally Wicked," or "TW," according to ECigIntelligence's research.

Ispire (ISPR) mentioned at its May '25 analyst call that "the UK led the European market by banning (e-cigarette) disposables." The expectation is that the rest of "EU would follow suit in due course," as per ISPR's comments.

TW's addition ensures that RLX remains relevant in an evolving regulatory environment.

The firm acknowledged at the second-quarter results meeting that "disposable products have historically been an integral part of our portfolio." But it noted that "we recognize that the future lies in more sustainable solutions." Its new daughter company's "full suite of capabilities and services in the local market" was also emphasized.

TW boasts a wide assortment of e-cigarettes, including vape kits that come with rechargeable batteries (to substitute for disposables). In my opinion, the acquiree will aid RLX's product mix optimization efforts. Separately, the United Kingdom can be the group's new growth engine.

Euromonitor published a report (not publicly available) titled "Smokeless Tobacco, E-Vapour Products and Heated Tobacco in the United Kingdom" in June 2025. TW's share of the sizeable $4 billion UK vape market is a mid-single-digit percent, as per this research organization's estimates.

RLX began venturing outside its home market, the PRC, in '23.

Non-Chinese operations only contributed slightly over a quarter of its top line last year. There's room for the enterprise to increase its overseas presence.

In the past, RLX's U.K. e-cigs were mainly sold via e-commerce sites or wholesalers.

TW has a network of 100+ physical outlets. The additional distribution channel is likely to translate into higher sales in this country. I also think it's a big difference to benefit from on-the-ground learnings regarding smokers' preferences.

RLX is exploring the possibility of entering "additional European countries" next year based on management's remarks at the Q2 call. The newly acquired UK business should act as a launchpad for its ambitions in this continent.

Potential Product Launches Have Huge Promise

The company is diversifying beyond e-vapor. It revealed at the late-Aug. earnings call that the "2B (To-Business) prototypes" for its "modern oral nicotine products (ONPs)" are done. The next step is introducing these items to "the 2C (To-Consumer) market." RLX's proposal could substantially widen its "Total Addressable Market," or "TAM."

UBS Group (UBS) issued a research note (not accessible in public) with the title "Global Tobacco: US Nicotine Survey" dated Sept. 5, 2025. The bank forecasts that sales of "nicotine pouches," a common form of oral nicotine, will rise +122% in the next five years to $10 billion in the United States by the end of this decade; this category is also predicted to account for a high-teens percentage of American cigarette consumption in volume terms.

My view is that these products should have favorable prospects around the world, because they cater to the needs of specific user segments.

RLX's Q2 analyst call commentary stressed that "oral nicotine fits seamlessly into the lifestyle of office workers and frequent travelers."

Most people have the experience of struggling to squeeze everything into suitcases as they pack for trips. You would assume that smokers prefer sachet-like nicotine pouches over vaping devices and related refillables in that kind of scenario.

On the other hand, white-collar workers have to be mindful of how they are judged by their bosses and colleagues, who might be non-smokers. ONPs can be easily consumed at the workstation, as they are smoke-free and don't emit any odors.

Also, "nicotine pouch users are (on average) 10 years younger than cigarette users," as indicated by UBS's early-September publication cited above. The financial institution's analysis found that these are perceived to be "less harmful nicotine products" by this demographic due to the absence of tobacco.

I see RLX's growth runway being extended with its entry into the ONP space.

You Should Consider These Variant Views

There may be post-M&A integration issues relating to the "Totally Wicked" deal. If RLX doesn't work well with TW's management, its future UK market performance might fall short of expectations.

RLX could take a longer-than-anticipated period of time to market its new ONP-related items. It is also possible that people choose to stay with tried-and-tested oral nicotine brands.

My Closing Thoughts

RLX's rating is revised from a "Hold" to a "Buy."

The group's outlook has become better, considering its recent acquisition and new product roadmap.

My fair price-to-sales valuation for RLX is 7.7 times by using its domestic peer Smoore (OTCPK:SMORF) (6969.HK) as a benchmark. This translates into a 48% (7.7/5.2) upside, taking into account the stock's current 5.2x P/S.

data.title: India Gold Market Update: Encouraging Start To Seasonal Demand
data.description:
Gold resumes climb

Gold prices saw a sharp upswing in late August, with the rally accelerating through the first half of September to hit fresh all-time highs, pushing year-to-date gains to 40%.1 International gold prices climbed 4% in August and added another 6.7% in early September2 as bullish sentiment deepened. Our Gold Return Attribution Model (GRAM) attributes August's gains to a weaker US dollar, elevated geopolitical tensions, and strong inflows into global gold ETFs. The continued momentum in September was supported by positive investor positioning – evidenced by rising futures net longs and sustained ETF inflows. Lower US Treasury yields, amid growing expectations of a Fed rate cut and concerns around the Fed’s independence, have provided additional tailwinds.

Chart 1: Sharp rally in gold prices

End of month LBMA Price PM and domestic spot price changes and movement*

Gold prices in India have closely mirrored international movements, with a weaker rupee (down 3% y-t-d) magnifying domestic price gains. As of mid-September, prices had risen 7.0% month-to-date3 to INR 106,863/10g, bringing the y-t-d increase to 44%. Notably, signs of demand interest have helped narrow domestic price discounts,4 which briefly flipped to a marginal premium in late August and again in mid-September. This marks a noteworthy change, as domestic gold prices had been trading at a near-sustained discount since December (Chart 2).

Chart 2: Domestic gold price discount narrows

NCDEX gold premium/discount relative to the international price*

Festive gold demand emerges, led by investment buying

Gold demand in India is showing signs of a pickup with the onset of the festive season, led primarily by a surge in physical investment demand for bars and coins, according to market reports and anecdotal evidence from trade channels. Investment interest is reportedly outpacing jewellery purchases, as consumers are drawn in by the renewed uptrend in prices and expectations of further increases.

Conversations with jewellery manufacturers and retailers suggest that jewellery demand, while present, remains uneven. High-value, wedding-related purchases have begun and are holding steady, while high prices have dampened lower-ticket daily-wear and discretionary buying, prompting a shift to lower carat products. Large retailers are reporting higher footfalls, supported by aggressive marketing and promotional campaigns,5 along with plans for new store openings. Smaller retailers, in contrast, continue to face muted demand. Robust exchange activity, where old gold jewellery is traded in for new, has also been a key contributor to overall sales. While volumes remain lower y/y, sales value has risen aided by the higher gold prices.

Bullion dealers have reportedly stepped up purchases since early September, likely in anticipation of stronger seasonal demand and a potential boost from increased consumer spending from the forthcoming reduction in Goods and Services Tax (GST).6 The tax cut on a range of items, including consumer goods, durables, and automobiles, takes effect from 22 September, just as the festive season enters its peak following a 16-day inauspicious period.7

Overall, expectations for a strong festive season are building across the gold trade.

ETFs: Sustained inflows and investor momentum

India’s gold ETFs saw a notable surge in inflows in August, marking the fourth consecutive month of positive growth. Cumulative net inflows totalled INR21.9bn (US$250mn), up 74% m/m, aligning closely with our earlier estimate.8 This surge is the second highest of the year, since January.9 The increase is likely driven by the sustained demand for safe-haven assets by investors seeking stability amid weak domestic equities and persistent global trade and geopolitical risks. Initial observations point to a continuation of this momentum, with strong positive net inflows during the first two weeks of September.10

Redemptions during the month were the lowest in seven months, totalling INR1.5bn (US$17mn) - a significant decline from the average redemption of INR7.5bn (US$83mn) recorded in the first seven months of the year. This drop in redemptions suggests that investors are adopting a longer-term perspective, choosing to stay invested rather than cashing out, despite the recent price gains. The total assets under management (AUM) in gold ETFs in India reached record highs of INR724bn (US$8.3bn), with holdings increasing by 2.1t to a total of 70t.

Along with strong inflows, investor participation also saw a significant increase, with 164 thousand new accounts (folios) added, pushing the total number of active folios to 8.03mn, a 24% increase since the start of the year.

Furthermore, a new gold ETF was launched in August,11 bringing the total number of gold ETFs listed in India to 22.12

Chart 3: Inflows gained pace in August

Monthly gold ETF flows in INRbn, and total holdings in tonnes*

RBI stays on hold

The RBI’s gold purchases have moderated in 2025, with no additions for the second straight month in August. Gold was bought in only three of the first eight months of the year, compared with near-consistent monthly additions through 2024. The scale of buying has also slowed sharply, with cumulative purchases at just 3.8t between January and August 2025, against 45.4t in the same period last year.

Despite this moderation, India’s gold reserves have climbed to a record 880t. While volumes rose by a modest 4% y/y, the valuation gains have been pronounced, with the value of the holdings up nearly 40%, on the back of higher gold prices. Gold now accounts for 12.5% of India’s foreign exchange reserves as of end August, up from 9% a year earlier. This highlights how price appreciation has significantly strengthened reserves, even amid restrained buying.

Gold imports rise signals resilient demand

India’s gold imports rose sharply in August, reaching a nine-month high and marking the second consecutive month of high imports, underscoring resilient domestic demand. Imports during the month totalled US$5.2bn, a 37% m/m increase, reflecting seasonal buying interest despite elevated prices. We estimate import volumes of 60-65t, up from 46t in July.

Chart 4: Imports rise to a multi-month high in August

Monthly gold imports in tonnes and US$bn*

Footnotes

1 Based on the LBMA Gold Price PM as of 15 September 2025.

2 1-15 September 2025.

3 Based on MCX Spot gold price PM as of 15 September 2025

4 The premium/discount of local gold prices is based on the LBMA Gold Price AM adjusted for import taxes and exchange rate, which is also referred to as the ‘landed price’

5 Jewellery brands boost festive advertising spends: Storyboard18.com, 05 September 2025.

6 GST council approves rates rationalisation: Ministry of Finance, 03 September 2025.

7 Pitru Paksha 2025 start and end date: Times of India, 09 September 2025.

8 WGC’s preliminary estimate, based on partial data, indicated net fund inflows of INR2,184cr (US$233mn).

9 Net fund inflows in January 2025 of Indian gold ETFs totalled INR3751cr (US$435mn).

10 Based on World Gold Council’s estimates of fund-wise net inflows.

11Angel One Gold ETF was launched in August 2025.

12A full list of the physically-backed gold ETFs we tracked can be found in: Gold ETF: Stock, Holdings and Flows | World Gold Council

Original Post

data.title: PFF Vs. HYG: Preferreds Stand To Gain More Than High-Yield At This Juncture
data.description:
Rate cuts have begun (–25 bps), but since the Federal Reserve was looking likely to ease monetary policy since the beginning of this year, more investors have been putting money into income funds. Two of these are the iShares iBoxx $ High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:HYG) and the iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF (NASDAQ:PFF), which I covered on October 25, 2022. At that time, I had a hold position mainly because of a higher-for-longer rate scenario, and indeed, PFF lost around 7% of its value by October 2023 before subsequently rising again.

The table below illustrates how high-yield has outperformed over the last three years, but more recent performance (three months) shows things are changing in favor of preference securities (preferreds).

Against such a backdrop, while highlighting the risks, this thesis aims to show that, in the current economic and monetary policy context, PFF is better for both yield and capital appreciation. I start by providing an overview of the similarities and differences in their underlying assets.

The Difference between these two Asset Classes

With assets under management of $14.79 billion, PFF is one of the largest funds focused on preferreds. It is managed by BlackRock's iShares and tracks the ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred & Hybrid Securities Index, which consists of preferred stocks and hybrid securities as shown below.

By hybrid, I mean that preferreds are traded on stock exchanges, hence classified as equity (meaning no maturity date), and whose prices can move in sync with the market. However, since they pay dividends, they also behave like fixed income, with the amounts known in advance. Moreover, they have priority over common stock dividends in the eventuality of the issuer becoming bankrupt, and through preferreds, one shares ownership of a company with other shareholders.

This hybrid nature differs from HYG, one of the largest funds of its kind, comprising an AUM of over $18.7 billion, which holds only corporate bonds, with a weighted average maturity of 3.47 years, as shown below. For this purpose, it tracks the Markit iBoxx USD Liquid High Yield Index and invests in U.S. dollar-denominated high-yield corporate bonds (junk bonds) issued by companies with lower credit ratings. As a result, they bear higher credit risk, implying investors need to be paid better yields than investment-grade loans to own them.

Therefore, PFF has some similarities with the bond ETF due to its fixed-income features, but it also exhibits equity-like qualities. However, they are both sensitive to interest rate variations.

Highlighting Rate Sensitivity and Credit Risks

PFF’s rate sensitivity is because its holdings pay fixed dividends just like the bonds held by HYG. Hence, similarly to bonds, the demand for preferreds drops when interest rates rise, and this was the case from March to December 2022 when the Federal Reserve aggressively tightened monetary policy to contain surging consumer prices. Hence, rising rates can negatively impact both ETFs, translating into capital losses.

Noteworthy, even when the U.S. Central Bank cut rates in August and September 2024, neither PFF nor HYG rallied because with policy rates still hovering near 5%, the market continued to price a “higher-for-longer” scenario. In such conditions, they must compete with long-duration treasury bonds offering similar or higher yields but which come with lower risk since they are issued by the government.

Conversely, during easing cycles, falling rates tend to boost preferreds and high-yield prices as their fixed dividends become more attractive. In this connection, with U.S. inflation contained at less than 3% for most of this year, it was becoming more likely for policy to be loosened. Hence, both funds have been rising, as illustrated by the chart below.

However, looking closer at the blue chart above, PFF has been gaining momentum versus HYG, and this trend could continue based on research by Cohen & Steers. According to them, historical data shows that investment-grade preferreds have performed well following the end of the U.S. Central Bank rate-hiking cycles, producing an average 12-month return of 14.2%.

In addition to rate sensitivity, preferreds are favored by the current economic context, as high-yield bonds also come with a higher risk of default. In this connection, HYG's holdings have an average credit rating of BB of 55% (table below) or the below investment-grade category, typically around BB or lower.

By contrast, PFF's average (53%) credit rating is around BBB, which is investment grade as shown below.

Therefore, PFF’s higher credit quality and lower default risk offer more stability, especially during periods of economic uncertainty.

In this connection, investors will note that PFF started outperforming HYG in August, which coincided with sharply higher unemployment figures for July. Hence, HYG’s lower-rated portfolio could face default risks in case of economic weakness. In contrast, PFF's preferreds are better positioned since they are not only investment grade but are also mainly issued by financial institutions (above 68%). These benefit from lower borrowing costs as policy is eased, in turn increasing risk-taking appetite, meaning more volume for banks' lending businesses.

Therefore, in case of further deterioration in employment numbers (currently a key factor being monitored by the Fed), combined with a high likelihood of rates being cut (possibly by 50 bps by the end of 2025), both ETFs will continue to rise, but PFF is likely to gain more. To obtain an idea of the potential appreciation, I consider preferreds' higher interest rate sensitivity.

PFF Could Appreciate more due to Higher Rate Sensitivity, but there are Risks

In this case, while both bonds and preferreds are sensitive to interest rate changes, the latter also have no fixed maturity date, as mentioned earlier, which implies a longer duration (perpetual) than HYG's high-yield (3.47 years).

While this sensitivity worked against PFF in 2022 when the Fed aggressively tightened policy by 400 basis points with a 15% decline, as shown below, it could play in its favor this time. Hence, in a sustained Fed easing cycle, if rates fall by around 200 basis points, its current share price of $32 could appreciate by around 7.5% (or half the 15% decline) to $34.4 (32 x 7.5%).

HYG could also gain, but to a lesser extent, or moderately benefit due to its shorter duration. Hence, based on its 11.43% loss in 2022, it could appreciate by roughly half (5.5%) to $85.5 (81 x 5.5%) based on its current share price of $81 in case the Fed eases by 200 basis points.

Thus, I am bullish on both ETFs, but there are risks.

First, PFF, being highly concentrated in the financial sector, at 68.2% of the overall portfolio weight, faces sector-specific risks. This is the reason it was more volatile during the 2023 banking crisis, which lasted from March to May 2023, as shown in the chart below. Second, it bears higher concentration risks, as it holds around 451 securities, with the top ten holdings comprising about 17% of its overall weight, as shown in the table below.

On the other hand, HYG, with around 1,316 securities, with the top 10 holdings comprising only 3.5% of assets, is more diversified. Also, HYG’s bonds are widely spread across different sectors of the economy, like communications, healthcare, and energy, instead of being dominated by financials like PFF.

However, despite being less concentrated, HYG is exposed to default risk (a higher probability of debtors not paying their dues because of their lower credit quality) in case of a prolonged economic slowdown or contraction. Hence, in addition to rates, high yield is highly sensitive to cyclicality risks, and if default rates rise, it can lead to investors exiting their positions, causing the high-yield ETF to lose value.

PFF Better for both Yield and Price Appreciation, but Higher-for-Longer could derail this Thesis

Moreover, in case the Fed does not continue to cut rates as expected because of sticky inflation, PFF's advantage is that its yield of 6.35% is higher than HYG's 5.69% while it charges less, or 0.45% versus 0.49% for high-yield, as shown below. Therefore, it can provide better mitigation against eroding returns through higher income and lower fees, two factors essential to navigate periods of prolonged volatility.

Moreover, in the current economic juncture, where the U.S. labor market has shown weakness for June, July, and August, signaling uncertainty in economic demand, PFF's shares, with their investment-grade quality, are better than HYG's BBB bonds, which are riskier (because of credit risks). Along the same lines, according to the latest annual stress test conducted by the Fed, the largest banks are well capitalized, making them highly likely to withstand an economic shock (extreme recession).

Now, to be fair to HYG, an important factor to consider is its higher liquidity (about 33.6M avg volume as shown below), or approximately ten times PFF's. In this case, its much higher liquidity means investors can buy or sell large amounts of shares quickly without significantly impacting the market price. This makes it ideal for tactical positioning as part of a broader bond portfolio during periods of market volatility. This is why I rate it as a buy.

In conclusion, the core driver behind this thesis is PFF’s higher rate sensitivity relative to HYG, favoring it in terms of price appreciation during the Fed's current easing cycle. Also, HYG, with its lower-graded bonds, is more sensitive to the deterioration in the U.S. employment numbers.

Moreover, as per the last FOMC meeting, inflation was mentioned as a concern since it is expected to remain elevated in 2026 at 2.6%. If this leads to slowing growth, the performance gap between PFF and HYG could widen, because this would likely force investors to demand extra yield for holding risky corporate bonds instead of safe U.S. treasuries of the same maturity. This will likely negatively impact HYG, while PFF’s higher-quality preferreds could be more resilient.

Ending on a cautionary note, inflation rising more than expected would result in a “higher-for-longer” scenario, which could derail this thesis.

data.title: Nvidia's China Problem: One Of The Risks Investors Can't Ignore
data.description:
In the last few years I have constantly been wrong about Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) and underestimated the growth potential for the business. In my last article about Nvidia, published in June 2025, I rated the stock as a Sell once again and still saw the stock as being overvalued. In the conclusion of my article, I wrote:

I still don’t see Nvidia as a “Buy” or a bargain and rather see the stock as a “Sell” with high downside risk. And while I don’t necessarily expect a steep decline for Nvidia anymore (although a price target of $30 is certainly a possibility), I don’t think the stock will move higher than $150 at this point and another decline to about $90 seems extremely likely and this is resulting in a more than 30% decline. Hence, I will still rate Nvidia as a “Sell” at this point and might change my mind when the stock to drop below $100 again.

Since my last article was published, the stock did not drop below $100 and actually moved much higher than $180. Since my last article was published, Nvidia increased its stock price by 22% and once again clearly outperformed the S&P 500. After Nvidia increased to almost $185, the stock is now correcting again and in the following article I will explain why I see more downside risk than upside potential at this point.

Quarterly Results

On August 27, 2025, Nvidia reported second quarter results for fiscal 2026, and the company did beat estimates for revenue as well as earnings per share once again. This is actually the 11th quarter in a row with Nvidia beating estimates for revenue as well as earnings per share.

While Nvidia did beat estimates once again, this is also the sixth quarter in a row with a lower growth rate. However, Nvidia still grew its revenue in the second quarter of fiscal 2026 by 55.6% year-over-year – from $30,040 million in Q2/25 to $46,743 million in Q2/26. Operating income increased by 52.6% year-over-year from $18,642 million in the same quarter last year to $28,440 million in this quarter. Diluted net income per share increased from $0.67 in Q2/25 to $1.08 in Q2/26 – resulting in 61.2% year-over-year growth.

When looking at the different business segments, we see all business segments contributing to growth. However, it is Data Center, which is still the dominant segment and responsible for growth in absolute numbers. Revenue for professional visualization increased 32.4% year-over-year to $601 million, and Automotive increased its revenue 69.4% year-over-year to $586 million. But combined, these two segments are only responsible for about 2.5% of total revenue.

The two main segments are Gaming, which increased its revenue by 48.9% year-over-year to $4,287 million in Q2/26, and especially Data Center, which increased its revenue 56.4% year-over-year to $41,096 million. Additionally, Data Center is responsible for 88% of total revenue and Nvidia is heavily dependent on this revenue stream.

But while these results are still great, I see more and more risks for Nvidia’s business – in the United States as well as abroad.

No More Revenue from China?

One of the problems, Nvidia might face in the coming quarters (and maybe years) is the situation in China. I already wrote about this problem in my last article about Nvidia as well as my last article about Alibaba (BABA), which was published last week. In the article, I wrote about the situation as Alibaba also announced it has developed a new chip, which is more versatile compared to its older chips. The company is not new to the design of semiconductors, and its last one was the Hanguang 800, which was released in 2019. I don’t know yet if I would call it a major news story, but it could have the potential to open a new source of revenue for Alibaba.

I also wrote in the article:

In the past, Alibaba was also one of the biggest customers of U.S. tech giant Nvidia (the H20 chip) but this year the company was running into regulatory hurdles to sell its products in China. In April 2025, the restrictions were tightened. In July, after Nvidia’s CEO was in talks with the U.S. administration, restrictions were loosened again, but now China was “ordering” its companies not to purchase chips from Nvidia anymore (citing security risks).

While this might create an opportunity for Alibaba, it might be a risk for Nvidia. In fiscal 2025, Nvidia generated $17.1 billion in annual sales in China (about 13% of the company’s total revenue).

It seems unlikely that revenue in China will decline to zero for Nvidia in the coming quarters, but the company had to report declining revenue in China in the second quarter of fiscal 2026: Quarterly revenue declined 24.5% year-over-year from $3,667 million in Q2/25 to $2,769 million in Q2/26.

Following strong growth rates in China in previous years (66% growth in fiscal 2025 and 78% growth in fiscal 2024) this is certainly not great news for Nvidia. The decisive question right now is how the situation will unfold: Will Nvidia be allowed to sell its chips? Will Chinese companies purchase these chips? What actions will the Chinese government take? During the last earnings call, CEO Huang commented on China:

The China market, I've estimated to be about $50 billion of opportunity for us this year if we were able to address it with competitive products. And if it's $50 billion this year, you would expect it to grow, say, 50% per year. As the rest of the world's AI market is growing as well.

It is the second largest computing market in the world, and it is also the home of AI researchers. About 50% of the world's AI researchers are in China. The vast majority of the leading open source models are created in China. And so it's fairly important, I think, for the American technology companies to be able to address that market. And open source, as you know, is created in one country, but it's used all over the world.

It seems difficult to make predictions about what might happen, as several scenarios are possible. Up until now, Nvidia’s chips still seem like the gold-standard and Chinese companies probably want to have the best chips available to be able to compete in the AI race. On the other hand, Chinese companies (as well as the Chinese government) probably will try everything to be less dependent on companies from the United States (and the U.S. government). And CEO Huang already made a statement about China moving forward in AI, which I like to repeat again:

China's AI moves on with or without U.S. chips. It has to compute to train and deploy advanced models. The question is not whether China will have AI, it already does. The question is whether one of the world's largest AI markets will run on American platforms. Shielding Chinese chipmakers from U.S. competition only strengthens them abroad and weakens America's position. Export restrictions have spurred China's innovation and scale. The AI race is not just about chips. It's about which stack the world runs on. As that stack grows to include 6G and quantum, U.S. global infrastructure leadership is at stake.

In the short term, Nvidia as well as China might lose. Nvidia will lose revenue; China might lose access to the best-in-class GPUs. However, in the long run, it seems like that China will figure out a way to replace Nvidia if this is necessary. And on Wednesday it was reported that the Cyberspace Administration of China has banned the nation’s largest technology companies – including Alibaba and ByteDance (BDNCE) – from buying Nvidia chips.

I don’t know if this is the end of the story in the ongoing trade war between China and the United States, but right now the outlook for Nvidia in China is rather bleak. Like I said, I don’t know if Chinese companies are able to offer a substitute for Nvidia’s H20 chip, but I also don’t know if the Chinese government will retreat its decision.

It is also true that the Chinese companies only have a fraction of the total generative AI market when looking at the foundation models (market share for Baidu (BIDU) and Alibaba combined was less than 5%). Nevertheless, the decisive question is which country and company will spend the most on AI and cloud infrastructure in the years to come – and probabilities are high that China will be number one.

U.S. Companies Spending Less?

However, it is not only China and Chinese companies that might pose a risk for Nvidia and lead to declining revenue. In the last few years, Nvidia’s revenue (and revenue growth) was mostly generated by the so-called “Hyperscalers” and leading tech companies Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG), Microsoft (MSFT) (in combination with OpenAI), Meta Platforms (META) and Amazon (AMZN).

Aside from these numbers by EpochAI, there is also other data available about who owns the most Nvidia H100 GPUs. And the question is if these companies will continue to invest heavily in GPUs in the years to come.

In my last article I already talked about the risk a potential recession might have on Nvidia. And although these companies have a lot of cash on the balance sheet and very little debt and therefore the potential to continue spending at a high pace – even during a recession – I don’t think this is a likely scenario. If a recession might hit the United States, these four companies will probably reduce spending.

A second risk for Nvidia is the question of how well the companies spending massively right now (especially Amazon, Alphabet, Meta Platforms and Microsoft (MSFT)) can capitalize on these investments. Without much doubt, all four companies are performing very well and are growing in the double digits. Nevertheless, there are also reports about many companies abandoning AI projects. S&P Global is writing:

The percentage of companies abandoning the majority of their AI initiatives before they reach production has surged from 17% to 42% year over year, with organizations on average reporting that 46% of projects are scrapped between proof of concept and broad adoption. This marks a significant departure from the previous downward trend, highlighting the difficulties many organizations face in rapidly deploying AI projects.

And this is a risk we certainly must take into account. These companies investing heavily in AI capacities also are listed companies and therefore must answer to the shareholders. As long as shareholders believe these investments are making sense, it won’t be a problem. But if sentiment might change, questions about declining free cash flow might arise.

And for these four companies, we can see that free cash flow is declining – in some cases only a little bit, in other cases it is declining in the double digits. So far, the combined revenue for the leading AI companies is still a fraction of the capital expenditures. OpenAI has reached a run rate of $10 billion in revenue, but it is still almost impossible at this point to justify the high capital expenditures by the current revenue.

Additionally, another problem could be the huge power demand for AI that might lead to some backlash. According to McKinsey, the global data center capacity demand will grow 3.5 times between 2025 and 2030 just due to the AI workload. Electricity prices in the United States are rising not only in the last twelve months (6.2% year-over-year increase), but growth accelerated especially in the last five years (more than 40% increase since 2020). And the reasons for growing electricity prices are certainly not only the high demand by data centers and AI, but policy decisions are also playing a role. Nevertheless, the question remains if the demand for electricity can be satisfied and if constantly higher electricity prices are accepted.

Finally, there is always the risk that competitors will finally be able to catch up to Nvidia (at least a little bit), and in my opinion, the 92% market share Nvidia obviously has in this market won’t last. Therefore, we also have to take into account the risk of Nvidia underperforming overall market growth as competitors might take market shares away from Nvidia, which might very well remain the dominant company. But it makes a difference if Nvidia has 92% market share or “only” 60%, for example.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

As always, let’s calculate an intrinsic value for the stock by using a discounted cash flow calculation. We are calculating with a 10% discount rate (as this is the annual return we like to achieve at least) and the last reported number of diluted shares outstanding, which was 24,532 million. The basis for our calculation can be the free cash flow of the last four quarters, which was $72,020 million.

In order to be fairly valued, Nvidia must grow its free cash flow around 19% annually for the next ten years, followed by 6% growth till perpetuity. And these are actually the growth rates analysts are expecting for the next ten years. According to analysts’ assumptions, earnings per share will grow with a CAGR of 19.1% between fiscal 2025 and fiscal 2035.

Although Nvidia seems fairly valued at this point, I would be more cautious with my assumptions due to the reasons above (revenue from China declining, U.S. companies decreasing CapEx again, and maybe Nvidia losing market share). Let’s assume Nvidia can grow its revenue 50% in fiscal 2026, 25% in fiscal 2027, and 10% in fiscal 2028 and the following years. Additionally, we assume that the free cash flow margin is declining from 45% in fiscal 2025, to 40% in fiscal 2026, 35% in fiscal 2027, and 30% in fiscal 2028 and the following years. When looking at the free cash flow margin in the past, a margin of 30% seems more reasonable than 45% or even 50%. The average in the last 10 years was a 30.7% free cash flow margin.

When calculating with these assumptions, we get an intrinsic value of $105.93 for Nvidia in this scenario, and this seems more reasonable in my opinion.

Conclusion

I will still rate Nvidia as a Sell at this point due to the much higher stock price at this point, and I still see the downside risk for Nvidia being bigger than the upside potential at this point. In a very optimistic scenario, Nvidia could be fairly valued, but I rather expect the stock price to be rather in the red area in the next few quarters.

Analysts are more optimistic, and the mean analyst estimate is $210 right now, which is still implying upside potential for Nvidia at this point. Nvidia is still not extremely overvalued at this point, and some other AI stocks are trading for much more insane valuation multiples, but Nvidia is still a Sell – especially due to the run the stock had in the last few months, which drove the stock price to unrealistic price levels again.

data.title: September's 5 Dividend Growth Stocks With Yields Up To 6%
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman

For some background on this monthly publication, here is my view on dividend growth stocks:

Dividend growth stocks aren't always the most exciting investments out there. They often aren't grabbing the headlines, and they aren't the stocks running up hundreds of percentages in a year. In fact, they are often some of the least exciting stocks. And that is precisely their strongest selling point. With such a vast world of dividend growth stocks available out there, it is important to screen through to see if there are any worthwhile investments to explore.

They are stocks that provide growing wealth over time to income investors. Dividend growers are often larger (not always), more financially stable companies that can pay out reliable cash flows to investors. Some are slower growers than others. Some are going to be cyclical that require a strong economy. Some are going to be secular, which doesn't generally rely on a more robust economy.

Dividend growth can promote share price appreciation. Of course, that is if these companies are growing their earnings to support such dividend growth in the first place. Trust me. There are yield traps out there - I've owned a few that I'm not particularly proud of.

I like to think of investing in dividend stocks as a perpetual loan of sorts. Essentially, every dividend is a repayment of your original capital. Eventually, holding long enough, you have the position "paid off." It is all returned back into your pocket from that point forward.

All of this being said, it is important to understand my approach to dividend stocks and why screening dividend stocks can be important for income investors. As with any initial screening, this is just an initial dive - more due diligence would be necessary before pulling the trigger.

The Parameters For Screening

I'll be using some handy features that Seeking Alpha provides right here on their website for this screen. In particular, I will be screening utilizing their quant grades in dividend safety, dividend growth and dividend consistency.

Dividend Safety is relatively self-explanatory. These will be stocks that SA quants show reasonable safety compared to the rest of their various sectors. The grade considers many different factors, but earnings payout ratios, debt and free cash flow are among these. This category will be stocks with A+ to B- ratings.

For the dividend growth category, we have factors such as the CAGR of various periods relative to other stocks in the same sector. Additionally, the quants also look at earnings, revenue and EBITDA growth. As we will see, this doesn't mean that every stock with a higher grade has the growth we are looking for. This just factors in that the dividend has grown or earnings are growing to support possible dividend growth. For these, the grades will also be A+ through B- ratings.

Finally, for dividend consistency, we want stocks that will be paying reliable dividends for us for a very long time. In particular, hopefully, they are raising yearly, though that isn't an explicit requirement. We will also include stocks with a general uptrend in dividend payments, which means there could have been periods where they paused increases for a year or two.

After looking at those factors alone, we are left with 423 stocks at this time, which is up from last month's 416. I'll link the screen here, though it is a dynamic list that constantly updates regularly. When viewing this article, there could be more or less when going to the link.

From there, I wanted to narrow down the list a lot more. I then sorted the list by TTM dividend yield, from highest to lowest. Since these will be safer dividend stocks in the first place, screening for those among the higher payers shouldn't hurt. Though there are always exceptions, after all, this is simply an initial screening, and I try to note those that I feel aren't as safe as the algorithms are suggesting.

As is usually the case, we have a few repeats that we recently covered, so we'll skip over those until at least a quarter has gone by before providing some quick updates. Those names include RLJ Lodging Trust (RLJ), United Parcel Service (UPS), Alpine Income Property Trust (PINE), Hess Midstream (HESM) and Alexandria Real Estate Equities (ARE).

Another name that made it toward the top of the list this month was Ennis (EBF)—while it might be an interesting investment opportunity, we'll skip over that name as well. It hasn't raised its regular quarterly dividend since 2021.

What that does leave us with is peculiar this month, as it is an interesting list of infrastructure plays, primarily in the energy space. Energy investments such as midstreams and MLPs often pay outsized dividends/distributions, which isn't anything new, but to see all five of the names today being energy infrastructure related hasn't happened before.

#1 HA Sustainable Infrastructure Capital (HASI) 6% Yield

This is the first time we've touched on HASI since initially starting this monthly screening piece, going back to December 2021. However, this isn't the first time I've run across HASI, as it is an interesting investment choice in the renewable infrastructure space. With that said, HASI is rather unique in the fact that it operates more like a fund than a traditional operating company. That is, they invest in different projects instead of operating the assets themselves. They are a rather large 'fund' with $14.6 billion in managed assets.

They boast over 1250 investments closed with over 100 client relationships. Of course, as their name would suggest, they are investing in projects such as solar and wind, among other projects. We all know energy demand around the world is only growing, and investing in renewable energy sources is going to be a part of providing that supply.

These investments have translated into predictable earnings and cash flow to pay out to their investors; since 2014, the adjusted EPS has seen a 10% CAGR.

Given the rising earnings, that's translating into a growing dividend for investors. Over the long term, they have a target of around a 50% payout ratio for the dividend, but they are guiding for 2027 to have the payout ratio closer to 55-60%. That means we should expect to see dividend growth slow in the coming years unless earnings surprise to the upside relative to their ~10% long-term estimates.

I believe that HASI is an interesting investment that could be worth exploring even further to see if it would be worthwhile.

#2 Enbridge (ENB) 5.53% Yield

ENB is a name we've come across before, as are the rest of the names we'll be touching on today. It is also one of the more popular energy infrastructure plays as a long-time dividend-growing machine from Canada. However, U.S. investors with a stronger USD haven't quite seen growth in the dividend like their Canadian counterparts due to currency fluctuations.

So Canadians see 30 consecutive years of dividend increases, and Americans would see what looks like flat dividends for the last several years now. For what it is worth, they are continuing to expect 5% growth through the end of the decade.

While the company operates through several different segments, the largest contributor to adjusted EBITDA is their liquids pipelines business. However, gas transmission and gas distribution & storage are also significant enough divisions to move the needle. Their renewable power segment doesn't contribute much, but that is expected to help provide overall earnings growth to continue to support the growing payout to investors.

#3 ONEOK (OKE) 5.52% Yield

OKE is another energy infrastructure play as a midstream company focused primarily on natural gas pipelines and based in the U.S. Their largest segment is natural gas liquids, followed by natural gas gathering and processing, but right behind that is refined products and crude. To a smaller degree, there are natural gas pipelines. That all contributes to 2025's adjusted EBITDA guidance of $8.225 billion.

Adjusted EBITDA growth for this company has been exploding over the last several years. Instead of this being organic growth from the business alone, it has been largely driven by sizeable acquisitions. The latest 2025 estimates are the full year of EnLink and Medallion acquisitions, now being reflected. Assuming no new acquisitions, 2026 estimates are still calling for growth, but that will be a bit more moderate.

As I've been writing covered calls on my TC Energy (TRP) investment, as it looks relatively more expensive than when I bought it several years ago, I've been keeping an eye on this space a bit more, looking for a potential replacement if need be. Interestingly, OKE was one of those names that made the list, as it looks like it has become beaten down.

EV/EBITDA favors OKE currently over TRP, which is one of the reasons it caught my attention as a potential attractively valued alternative. Forward EV/EBITDA is 9.76x for OKE and 13.2x for TRP.

Similar to ENB, OKE boasts a long history of growing its dividend—though it did stall out during the Covid pandemic. That isn't necessarily a negative, in my opinion, as keeping a strong balance sheet is important during times of such uncertainty and potentially being overextended.

As always, this is only an initial screening, and more digging would need to be done to see if OKE would be a decent potential replacement for TRP. However, it is one of my primary considerations at this time.

#4 Pembina Pipeline Corp. (PBA) 5.30% Yield

With PBA, we are back in Canada with another energy transportation and midstream services company. They used to be more unique as they offered a monthly distribution compared to most of their peers, who paid quarterly. However, they transitioned to being a boring quarterly payer again:

Finally, we announced yesterday our intention to move from a monthly to a quarterly common share dividend payment in 2023, payments to be made in March, June, September and December of each year. This change aligns Pembina's dividend practices with the vast majority of its peers and companies within the TSX 60. Subject to approval by the Board of Directors, the monthly dividend is expected to end with the dividend to be declared in early December and paid on December 30. The first quarterly dividend is expected to be effective for the dividend to be paid in March 2023.

One benefit could be keeping cash for longer, earning some interest, but otherwise, I'm not sure why being similar to the vast majority of its peers is a strength. Either way, it is a done deal after decades of them paying out monthly previously. Additionally, similar to ENB, for U.S. investors, due to the CAD/USD currency conversion, it would appear as though they haven't raised their dividend for the last several years.

Instead, we know they've been growing in an upward trend in their annual dividend since 2011. Similar to OKE, they stalled out with consecutive growth in 2020/2021, where they held the annual payout at $2.52.

PBA has made our list previously, but it hasn't been since October 2022. It doesn't necessarily provide the highest yield, but it has been similar to its peers in being able to provide reliable growth over the years. On the valuation front, the EV/EBITDA comes in at 12.15x, and the forward EV/EBITDA comes in at 10.49x. On that basis, it makes it relatively more attractively valued than TRP but not as attractive as OKE.

#5 Canadian Natural Resources Limited (CNQ) 5.20% Yield

Finally, we have CNQ—another Canadian operation, as the name would imply. This isn't the first time this name has shown up, and the last time was just back in March of this year. Since then, shares have seen a small rise, but over the last year, shares have still gone down by over 10%.

Unlike the other names we touched on today, this is going to be a relatively more volatile name, as it isn't a pipeline play but an upstream operation in the gathering and processing space.

That leaves them generally more subject to commodity price fluctuations, whereas pipeline operators typically have high levels of fixed-fee contracts in place to help provide more predictability. CNQ provides an estimate of free cash flow per share based on the price of WTI, and that can start to show us just how impacted the company can be.

However, CNQ is quite diversified in its production mix. That can help to limit some of the negative impacts that one commodity price, such as natural gas, collapses and stays depressed.

It also means that in good times, CNQ can do quite well in terms of its share price appreciating and being able to deliver significant dividend growth.

The growth in the dividend has been significant enough now that even with the currency fluctuations between CAD/USD, it still looks like it is growing for U.S. investors. They paid a significant special dividend in 2022 and, even with being a more volatile business, have managed to have 25 years of consecutive dividend increases.

At a ~21% CAGR since the company's inception, it has been at a significant pace as well. However, the growth has been in more recent years, with the last 5-year CAGR at over 22.5% compared to 15.6% over the last decade. Of course, that is quite impressive nonetheless.

In terms of valuation, it isn't going to be able to be directly compared to the pipeline companies we touched on above. That said, for some context, EV/EBITDA here is 6.05x, with forward EV/EBITDA at 5.98x. This can tell us that investors are seemingly unwilling to pay up for something that is higher risk, such as CNQ; at least that is my impression. However, it also tells us that there isn't too much growth expected going forward since the EV/EBITDA multiple is staying rather flat.

For investors who can handle more risk, CNQ seems to be quite a strong candidate to consider. They've certainly been able to deliver strong and consistent dividend growth.

data.title: SCHG: This Is What The Final Stages Of A Bull Market Look Like
data.description:
My previous bearish article on the Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF (NYSEARCA:SCHG) didn't age well. In less than three months, the ETF gained another 9.32%. Back then, I stated that SCHG's high concentration in overvalued stocks mirrors past bubbles. I also cautioned readers that we're likely in the latter stages of a long bull market that might lead to disappointing returns going forward.

What's changed since then that justifies revisiting the thesis:

  • Average valuations are even higher than before, rising from a 36x P/E ratio to a 41x.
  • Concentration risk has further increased, as the Magnificent Seven take up an even larger share.
  • Investor sentiment has become more exuberant, as believers slowly start to outweigh the doubters.

Back then, I also didn't mention that the final stage of a long bull market tends to end parabolically. This bull market has been going on for more than 16 years. It could go on for another couple of years, and if history is any guide, the final years tend to be the most profitable for nimble short-term-oriented speculators, as the masses are finally convinced that "this time it's really different".

As a long-term value investor, I stay away from euphoria. If an investment doesn't make sense from a mathematical basis, it's not for me. Well, knowing that I may miss out on a few quick gains.

Just because the markets continue to rise doesn't mean old valuation metrics have become obsolete. The higher the valuations we pay, the more risks we face.

SCHG's average earnings multiple keeps rising, and the expected returns are therefore lower than before. Because of those developments, the argument remains similar—but the urgency and risks are now more pronounced.

We're living in extraordinary times. The S&P 500 and NASDAQ seem unstoppable, and the more the markets rise, the more believers they convert.

The major indices are reaching valuation levels last seen in the late 1990s. It ended in the dot-com crash and the lost decade—a decade of negative nominal returns.

While I don't predict another lost decade (nor can I rule it out), the higher valuations we pay, the higher the risks we face, and the fewer returns we can expect.

Yet, in the short term markets don't behave on a mathematical and statistical basis. The longer a bull market rages on, the more investors start believing that it'll go on forever. The average investor becomes bullish at exactly the wrong time.

I clearly remember the sentiment ten years ago. Back then, there was a widespread fear that markets were in a bubble. I got asked if it's time to sell out.

My response was simple: "Markets usually don't crash when everyone is expecting it. Only when most investors become complacent is it time to worry".

Ten years later, and valuations are in the stratosphere. It seems like fewer investors worry about valuations, even though they have become more worrisome.

Long bull markets typically end in euphoria. In other words, the final stages are often the most fun. People quit their day jobs to participate in day trading. Endless prosperity is not only a possibility but a certainty.

Given the current sentiment, the major indices might still have room to run, long bull markets usually end parabolically. But it could also end tomorrow, and that's the risk.

The best advice I can give to long-term investors is to dampen your expectations for the next decade, and most importantly, don't sell out when optimism turns into widespread fear.

With that in mind, let's put the doom and gloom briefly aside and dive deeper into the Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG).

Schwab US Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG)

SCHG offers access to large-cap U.S. equities that exhibit growth style characteristics, according to Schwab.

The net expense ratio is 0.04% and the turnover rate is 3.00%. Both are low; therefore, SCHG is a low-cost ETF, which I view favorably as a long-term-oriented investor. But there are "hidden" fees—namely, the valuations you're paying for the underlying holdings at current prices.

P/E Ratio

Yahoo Finance claims the P/E ratio is 35.91, while the Wall Street Journal has it at 40.89. Different providers calculate the P/E ratio differently, but I usually rely on the WSJ numbers, as they tend to reflect the earnings multiple on a TTM basis quite accurately.

A 41x earnings multiple is steep. The last time the NASDAQ was trading at similar valuations was in 1998. Back then, there were some warning voices, but over the short term, they were wrong.

From 1998 to early 2000, the NASDAQ gained more than 300%, only to decline by 80% in the two years thereafter.

Long-term investors, who bought growth stocks in 1998, had to wait for more than a decade to achieve positive nominal total returns. That's the risk we're facing by paying more than 40x earnings, but over the short term, it could go either way. Over the medium term, there's a heightened risk of dismal total returns.

The biggest risk is selling out at exactly the wrong time, a common error made by the majority of investors.

"Everybody has a plan until they get punched in the face" - Mike Tyson

We plan to invest for the long term, but if challenges arise, the long term can become the short term. By mentally preparing for the likely challenges ahead, we could belong to the small group of successful investors who see opportunity in a bear market at a time when others are panicking. Never waste a good crisis.

Dividend Yield

Growth investors usually don't care about dividends as long as prices keep rising. It's only when greed turns into fear that dividends become important again. Right now, SCHG is yielding 0.43%, which can be described in one word: negligible.

We're in the Latter Stages of a Bull Market

As investors, we bet on probabilities, not on certainties. We're living in an uncertain world with an uncertain future. That's not a popular thing to say as an analyst, but it's the truth. And the truth will set you free.

All we can do is to estimate the odds and bet when the odds are in our favor. Right now, the odds don't seem to be in our favor when it comes to ETFs like SCHG. The markets are showing signs of excess. Worthless altcoins with very little, if any, use value are trading at more than a trillion-dollar market cap.

A seemingly declining car company with high fixed costs is demanding a triple-digit P/FCF ratio. An AI software company is trading at 250x P/FCF. The NASDAQ and S&P 500 are trading at valuation levels only seen once in over a century, the ones in the late 1990s.

Those are not the characteristics of a cautious environment but the latter stages of a bull market.

This bull market started in March 2009. It has been going on for 16 years. Even if we're in the latter stages, it could go on for another 1 or 2 years. Maybe even longer if we were to reach Nikkei 1989 levels, which peaked around 60 to 70 times earnings. If the S&P 500 were to rise to those levels, we'd witness another 100% gain.

But the higher this bull market peaks, the more painful the following bear market will likely be. And as long as the world is still cyclical and recessions and economic turmoil aren't a thing of the past, there will be downturns.

It took the Nikkei roughly 30 years to bottom after the excesses in 1989. The U.S. indices fared better after the dot-com bust, but a decade of negative nominal returns is still painful. Again, most investors sold out and made temporary losses permanent.

The biggest risk over the coming decade isn't tariffs or geopolitics, it's overpaying and multiple compression.

Similarities to the Nifty Fifty Bubble

As mentioned before, as investors, we're betting on probabilities, not certainties. I could be wrong.

The Magnificent Seven have shown annual earnings growth of 20%+ over the last few years. If this growth continued, the valuations right now could be considered reasonable.

Given the size of the companies, the large investments in AI without a clear path to decent ROIC over the medium term, as well as the fact that we're living in a cyclical world, I view endless growth as a small probability, but nonetheless, it is a probability.

It's not the first time that investors believed in endless prosperity. Back in the early 1970s, there was a group of roughly 50 large-cap stocks called the Nifty Fifty. They were considered "one-decision stocks", you bought them and never sold.

Just like the Magnificent Seven, they were growing at 20%+ annually, and investors expected them to grow endlessly. "Trees don't grow to the sky" is a famous saying, but the more prices rise, the more investors seem to forget.

At the height of the bubble, some Nifty Fifty stocks were trading at triple-digit P/E ratios, but the average stock was trading at 42x earnings.

Then the 1973-1974 recession came, growth slowed down, and investors started to panic. A small change can lead to staggering losses when we're paying high multiples.

Many of the Nifty Fifty stocks declined by 70%, 80%, 90% or more. In less than two years, the average P/E ratio fell into the single digits.

That would have been the perfect time to load up on the Procter & Gambles, the Coca-Colas, and the Johnson & Johnsons of this world, but only a few brave souls (including Warren Buffett) took advantage of it. Most investors bought into the hype and sold out into the fear. If you only extract one lesson from this article, don't let that be you!

Sector Allocation

The short history lesson was necessary to understand what kind of environment we're in. With that in mind, let's continue on SCHG.

Not surprisingly, as a growth ETF, SCHG is heavily exposed to tech. It's the largest sector, with 48.61% of total assets invested. Tech companies can also be found under communication or consumer cyclical, so the allocation could be higher.

Top 10 Holdings

SCHG holds 230 stocks, and the top 10 holdings represent 57.28% of total assets. It's basically a concentrated bet on just a few names trading at elevated multiples. What could go wrong?

NVIDIA leads the list with 11.23% of assets invested. It's followed by Microsoft, Apple, Amazon, and Broadcom.

A Few Words on the Top Holdings

If you're paying an average of 42x earnings, there better be decent growth ahead; otherwise, you're vastly overpaying. NVIDIA is the largest holding, which, to me, is worrying. To quote from one of my previous articles:

AI is still in its infancy, let's call it the gold rush phase. So far, NVIDIA holds a strong position in supplying the shovels to the miners seeking their fortune, but the basics of our capitalistic system shouldn't be forgotten. Whenever there are outsized returns, competition is at your back, and I wouldn't bet on NVIDIA holding the same position years from now.

Also, the AI boom could slow down substantially during the next recession, or if the players find out that it isn't as profitable as they once hoped it would be.

Whenever there are revolutionary technologies showing up, like the internet, railroads, canals, and now AI, there's usually an initial investing boom where the crowd gets excited. This is usually followed by a huge correction, like we saw during 2000-2003 after the dot-com exuberance.

If you're really a believer in AI, the next large crash will be the opportunity to load up on your favorite shares. But having experienced the markets long enough, whenever prices are low, there just aren't that many believers around.

As a long-term-oriented investor, I find it highly unlikely that NVIDIA can keep its high margins and growth rate over the long term. Yet, when you're paying a 50x P/E ratio, that's already priced in. If the growth doesn't materialize, look out below.

I also find it unlikely that companies like Apple, Microsoft, Amazon, and Meta will be able to sustain their growth rates over the long term. Size matters for growth—ask Berkshire Hathaway. The next recession will likely be painful for stocks trading above 40x earnings multiples. A lot of the (potential) growth has already been priced in.

Tesla's car sales have been declining rapidly, and the brand seems permanently damaged, yet the stock price doesn't seem to care and is trading at over 200x earnings. A clear sign of exuberance and excess in the markets. Over the short term, narratives win. Over the long term, fundamentals win.

Comparison Against Other ETFs

Let's compare SCHG's total returns to its peers. The best advice I can give to investors is to stop extrapolating the past into the future. "Past performance is not indicative of future returns"—what if it's true?

Since the beginning of the bull market in 2009, growth stocks have performed exceptionally well. SCHG leads the list on a total return basis, which makes it a popular choice for growth investors.

The great performance of the past has led to elevated valuations in the present, which could lead to disappointing returns in the future.

ETFs like SCHG will likely be much higher than now decades from now, but in between, there will be painful bear markets. The higher the valuations you pay, the more likely you'll get caught in one.

At current valuations, I can't help but rate it a "sell". This rating doesn't mean long-term investors should sell out. Missing the reentry point and potential capital gains taxes are all important factors to consider.

The higher the capital gains taxes, the less it makes sense for long-term investors to sell down, even though valuations appear risky at this point. When it comes to entering new positions, I'd wait for better entry points at lower valuations.

This article is just a little reminder that it might be time to dampen our expectations for the coming decade, and most importantly, prepare mentally so we don't sell out due to misleading expectations that weren't met.

Conclusion

Investing is less about predicting the next move and more about positioning to thrive across market cycles. There's no doubt that SCHG holds some of the best companies in the world, but even great businesses can be poor investments at the wrong price.

The current valuations are mirroring the valuation levels in the late 1990s and during the Nifty Fifty bubble in the early 1970s. While history doesn't repeat, it often rhymes.

If you're a long-term investor, that doesn't mean panic. It means preparing mentally for a market that may disappoint over the next decade. In the short term, it could go either way, but there's a heightened chance for dismal returns over the medium term. It might be wise to preserve capital to deploy it whenever better opportunities arise.

data.title: Markets Still Expect Lower Yields After Fed Rate Cut
data.description:
Thank you, sir, may I have another?

That’s the main takeaway for markets-based rate cut expectations following the Federal Reserve’s widely expected ¼-point cut for its target rate announced on Wednesday. Although key Treasury yields popped yesterday (suggesting anxiety about rate cuts when inflation is edging higher and is well above the Fed’s 2% target), market signals still point to expectations that the Fed will continue to ease monetary policy. The driving factor for the Fed’s decision: a slowing labor market, which the central bank sees as the bigger risk at the moment vs. inflation.

Explaining the cut, the Fed’s FOMC statement advised: “Recent indicators suggest that growth of economic activity moderated in the first half of the year. Job gains have slowed, and the unemployment rate has edged up but remains low. Inflation has moved up and remains somewhat elevated.”

The central bank’s revised rate outlook, released yesterday as part of new economic projections, anticipates the median Fed funds rate (at 4.125% after yesterday’s cut) will continue sliding. The ”projected appropriate policy path” for the target rate for 2025 is 3.6%, slipping further to 3.4% for 2026 - moderately below the current 4.125% median.

Fed funds futures are pricing in another ¼-point cut at the next policy meeting in Oct, and a repeat performance is expected for the December meeting. By the end of the year, futures are pricing in high odds that the target range will drop to a 3.50%-to-3.75% range, in line with the Fed’s current outlook.

The Treasury market also sees more rate cuts brewing, based on the policy-sensitive 2-year Treasury yield. Although this widely followed rate rose sharply yesterday to 3.57%, it remains well below the current median Fed funds rate - a gap that suggests the market is still expecting rate cuts.

A simple model that compares the current median Fed funds rate to the sum of the unemployment rate and the annual pace of headline consumer inflation (a proxy for monitoring conditions re: the Fed’s dual mandate) suggests that policy is still modestly tight. In turn, that leaves room for another rate cut to align policy with a neutral level vs. current economic conditions.

A possible joker in the deck is the risk that inflation will continue to edge up - a risk that Fed Chairman Powell noted in his press conference yesterday:

We have begun to see goods prices showing through into higher inflation, and actually the increase in goods prices accounts for most of the increase in inflation, or perhaps all of the increase in inflation over the course of this year. Those are not very large effects at this point, and we do expect them to continue to build over the course of the rest of the year and into next year.

For now, the Fed sees the slowdown in hiring as a bigger threat. “It’s really the risks that we’re seeing to the labor market that were the focus of today’s decision,” Powell explained.

But as I discussed yesterday at TMC Research, the rise in core consumer inflation in recent months, based on the annualized 3-month change, could be a warning sign that pricing pressure will accelerate further. If so, expectations for more rate cuts may be premature.

For the moment, markets are still pricing in more rate cuts. The question is whether incoming inflation numbers will persuade the crowd to reprice expectations?

data.title: Goldman Sachs Emerging Markets Equity Insights Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

In the second quarter of 2025, the MSCI Emerging Markets Index rose by 11.99% amid significant volatility caused by tariff-related announcements on Liberation Day. Asian Emerging Markets were particularly affected but later recovered, slightly outpacing developed markets. Domestic investors seized this as a buying opportunity, increasing their exposure, while foreign institutional investors (FIIs) bought around $30 billion from April lows, reversing about one-third of their previous year's selling¹ As mentioned, East Asia saw a strong recovery, with Taiwan and Korea both rising over 30%, outperforming the broader EM index by 15-20%.¹ Geopolitical tensions, especially between India and Pakistan and in the Middle East, had limited impact on this recovery.

Performance Review

In Q2 2025, the Goldman Sachs Emerging Markets Equity Insights Fund returned 11.61%, underperforming the MSCI Emerging Markets Index by 38bps (net, I-share).

Our Themes and Trends investment pillar was the largest contributor to excess returns across the quarter. Notably, our overweight positioning to Korean Aerospace & Defense stocks proved additive to excess returns, as these names benefitted from the broad trend of European rearmament amid fears of a US defense pullback.

The Sentiment Analysis investment pillar was relatively flat across the period, with positioning contributory to excess returns in the same Korean Industrials names offsetting losses from our overweight position in Chinese Consumer Discretionary names, which struggled in the quarter amid weak domestic consumer demand.

Our High-Quality Business Models pillar was also quite flat across the quarter, with our overweight towards Taiwanese Semiconductor names, which performed strongly amid surging global demand for AI and high-performance computing chips, helping offset other losses.

The Fundamental Mispricings investment pillar had the most challenging quarter. This was in large part due to positioning in Indian Information Technology names which were challenged due to their revenue exposure to the United States – especially following the Trump administration’s Liberation Day announcements.

Country Tilts proved additive to performance in the period due to an overweight towards Mexico, whose equity markets benefitted from constructive Central Bank policies and a more muted impact from US tariffs than expected.
